Catalog Description

Computer Organization. (3-2). Credit 4. Computer systems from programmer's

perspective including simple logic design, data representation and processor

architecture, programming of processors, memory, control flow, input/output, and

performance measurements; hands-on lab assignments. Prerequisite: Grade of C

or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent enrollment.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent

enrollment.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Perform Basic Logic Design

Understand the design of a simple computer system

Understand elements that make up a CPU (Central Processing Unit)

Design software with an understanding of how it will run on computers

Participate in discussions that involve impact of hardware and software

elements on each other and the performance of the program

Take intermediate and advanced classes in computer architecture,

systems, operating systems, cloud, parallel computing

Program for performance, including efficiently using the cache hierarchy

Special Course Designation

None

This material Is: Required

Computer Systems: A Programmer's Perspective

ISBN:  978-0134092669

Authors:  Randal E. Bryant and David O'Hallaron

Publisher:  Pearson

Notes:

Grading Policy

70%; D is ≥ 60%; F is < 60%.

Homework Assignments:  (10% of grade). We'll have several homework

assignments, many requiring programming.

Exams:  (70% of grade). There will be several written exams throughout

There is no extra credit.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Late work is not accepted. Work submitted by a student as makeup work for

an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempted from the

late work policy.

Miscellaneous

AI Statement

AI is so good these days that I can’t tell if you’re using it. If I tell you not to

use it, it makes it difficult for you because you suspect your classmates will

do it anyway, so why shouldn’t you do it too if you want to get a good grade?

Rather, I try to reduce the incentives to use it. So the homeworks are worth

only 10%, while the exams are worth 70%, and you can’t use AI on the

exams because I don’t allow devices in the exams. If you use AI to do the

homework for you, you might get that full 10% for the homeworks but you

won’t learn what you need to learn to pass the exams so you’ll fail anyway. If

you get stuck on a homework assignment, feel free to consult AI to help you

as you would a teaching assistant or instructor, but make sure you’re

actually learning the material rather than letting the AI just do it for you.
